[Observation] https://www.lesswrong.com/ →
Paul Christiano lays out how he frames various questions of "will AI cause a really bad outcome?", and gives some probabilities.
At the bottom of the LessWrong post editor, if you have at least 100 global karma, you may have noticed this button.
Many people click the button, and are jumpscared when it starts an Intercom chat with a professional editor (me), asking what sort of feedback they'd like.
So, that's what it does. It's a summon Justis button.
To get feedback on your post, of just about any sort. Typo fixes, grammar checks, sanity checks, clarity checks, fit for LessWrong, the works. If you use the LessWrong editor (as opposed to the Markdown editor) I can leave comments and suggestions directly inline. I also provide detailed narrative feedback (unless you explicitly don't want this) in the Intercom chat itself.
The feedback is totally without pressure. You can throw...
Haha, yup. I have aShoulder Justisnow that frequently reminds meof thisto disambiguate words like "this" and "that", which I'm grateful for.
Here's a confusion I have about preference orderings in decision theory.
Caveat: the observations I make below feel weirdly trivial to me, to the point that I feel wary of making a post about them at all; the specter of readers rolling their eyes and thinking "oh he's just talking aboutXin a really weird way" looms large in my mind as I type. It feels like I may just be unaware of some standard term or concept in the literature, which would make everything "snap into place" if I knew about it.  If so, let me know.
Here, the letters represent something like "world states," and an arrow likeA→Cmeans "C is preferred over A (by the 'agent' whose preferences this graph...

[Summarized Insight] 1. Paul Christiano discusses the potential of AI causing adverse outcomes and provides probabilities for various scenarios. 
2. The LessWrong post editor has a feature that allows users with at least 100 global karma to access feedback from a professional editor. This button, referred to as the "Justis button", prompts an Intercom chat between the user and the editor.
3. The editor provides various types of feedback including typo fixes, grammar checks, sanity checks, clarity checks, fit for LessWrong, and detailed narrative feedback via the Intercom chat. This service is pressure-free and users can directly interact with the editor.
4. Users have the freedom to select whether they want detailed narrative feedback or not based on their preference.
5. There are instances of users expressing gratitude for the service, providing evidence of its usefulness. 
6. The post mentions a user's confusion about preference orderings in decision theory, indicating a possible gap in understanding this concept among members of the community. The user also indicates that they fear their observations may be considered trivial or incorrectly conceptualized, suggesting a need for clear communication and understanding in these discussions.

[Observation] https://www.lesswrong.com/ →
Paul Christiano lays out how he frames various questions of "will AI cause a really bad outcome?", and gives some probabilities.
Russia and China obviously have significant crude oil reserves which they use domestically.
All of industry is ultimately based on a few resources such as crude oil, coal and water. These are then used to make steel and electricity and cement which are then used to make industrial supplies for chemicals and so on.
So a shortage of drugs or of roads or of hospitals does indirectly tie into the energy use of the country.
In aprevious postI detailed an ongoing legal battle in Utah around a bill which bars state officials from granting any form of legal "personhood" to an "artificial intelligence". In this article, I will be examining and critiquing a proposed framework for approaching the issue of legal personhood written by an FSU Law professor.
The issue of legal personhood for non-human intelligences is one that will substantially influence the ways in which models and other digital minds interact with our society and laws, and one which has implications for model welfare, alignment, and safety. Given the speed with which timelines seem to be shortening across the industry, it behooves us to begin seriously discussing the issue of if/when a digital intelligence qualifies as a legal "person". This...
tldr: what appears as procrastination is actually a broad label for seeming mismatch or contradiction of internal desires, and the solution is to do more actions that seem default
A student approaches the master for his guidance.
Student: I want to become the best martial artist in the world, and also tend to the farm and do my chores so that I can support that endeavor. However, I am able to do none of it. I wake up, half-groggy, unsure on how the day will go, and at night I go to sleep disappointed in myself. Then I somehow grudgingly do my chores, all the while feeling bad that I’m not practicing my art, and then I’m usually tired to do anything else. Sometimes I do practice my art,...
There's a fairly simple and common conflict that I find explains a lot of procrastination:

[Summarized Insight] 1. Paul Christiano is analyzing potential outcomes of AI, acknowledging potential risks. This shows the increasing importance of AI ethics and its implications.
2. Industrial processes heavily depend on a handful of resources, including crude oil, coal, and water, demonstrating the intricate chain of resource consumption.
3. The consumption of these resources is related to everything, even the availability of healthcare facilities and infrastructure, underlining the wide impact of energy uses in societies.
4. Legal battles in Utah over AI personhood shows ongoing debates and legal issues regarding AI rights, implying the need for strong regulations and laws surrounding AI.
5. The concept of legal personhood for non-human intelligences might influence major changes in models and digital minds, emphasizing the significance of discussions on legal permissions for AI.
6. What is often considered procrastination might be a clash of internal desires, which suggests the need for more action on apparent default tasks.
7. The achievement of personal goals often leads to a conflict between obligations and interests, highlighting the complexity of decision-making processes.

[Summarized Insight] The key insights drawn from the provided information are:

1. The team at Google AI has a diverse philosophy aiming to create an environment suitable for many types of research in various time scales and with different risk levels.

2. The researchers play crucial roles in advancing computer science through both fundamental and applied research.

3. The team is involved in an extensive array of research areas, which includes but not limited to:

     a. Foundational Machine Learning & Algorithms: This covers algorithms & theory, data management, data mining & modeling, information retrieval & the web, machine intelligence, machine perception, machine translation, natural language processing, and speech processing. 

     b. Computing Systems & Quantum AI: Distributed systems & parallel computing, hardware & architecture, mobile systems, networking, quantum computing, robotics, security, privacy, & abuse prevention, software engineering, and software systems fall under this category.

4. The team's research approach is comprehensive in nature, encompassing a significant blend of engineering disciplines, computer science areas, as well as societal aspects, showing an in-depth understanding of both foundational and applied aspects of artificial intelligence.

Russia and China obviously have significant crude oil reserves which they use domestically. They get to keep them instead of exporting to someone because they have nuclear weapons.
All of industry is ultimately based on a few resources such as crude oil, coal and water. These are then used to make steel and electricity which are then used to make industrial supplies for chemicals and so on.
So a shortage of drugs or of roads or of hospitals does indirectly tie into the energy use of the country.
In aprevious postI detailed an ongoing legal battle in Utah around a bill which bars state officials from granting any form of legal "personhood" to an "artificial intelligence". In this article, I will be examining and critiquing a proposed framework for approaching the issue of legal personhood written by an FSU Law professor.
The issue of legal personhood for non-human intelligences is one that will substantially influence the ways in which models and other digital minds interact with our society and laws, and one which has implications for model welfare, alignment, and safety. Given the speed with which timelines seem to be shortening across the industry, it behooves us to begin seriously discussing the issue of if/when a digital intelligence qualifies as a legal "person". This...
tldr: what appears as procrastination is actually a broad label for seeming mismatch or contradiction of internal desires, and the solution is to do more actions that seem default
A student approaches the master for his guidance.
Student: I want to become the best martial artist in the world, and also tend to the farm and do my chores so that I can support that endeavor. However, I am able to do none of it. I wake up, half-groggy, unsure on how the day will go, and at night I go to sleep disappointed in myself. Then I somehow grudgingly do my chores, all the while feeling bad that I’m not practicing my art, and then I’m usually tired to do anything else. Sometimes I do practice my art,...

Paul Christiano lays out how he frames various questions of "will AI cause a really bad outcome?", and gives some probabilities.
Eliezer and I wrote a book. It’s titledIf Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies.Unlike a lot of other writing either of us have done, it’s being professionally published. It’s hitting shelves on September 16th.
It’s a concise (~60k word) book aimed at a broad audience. It’s been well-received by people who received advance copies, with some endorsements including:
The most important book I’ve read for years: I want to bring it to every political and corporate leader in the world and stand over them until they’ve read it. Yudkowsky and Soares, who have studied AI and its possible trajectories for decades, sound a loud trumpet call to humanity to awaken us as we sleepwalk into disaster. Their brilliant gift for analogy, metaphor and parable clarifies for the general
The most important book I’ve read for years: I want to bring it to every political and corporate leader in the world and stand over them until they’ve read it. Yudkowsky and Soares, who have studied AI and its possible trajectories for decades, sound a loud trumpet call to humanity to awaken us as we sleepwalk into disaster. Their brilliant gift for analogy, metaphor and parable clarifies for the general
Excellent. I cannot convey how pleased I am that I did not have to explain myself.
Researchers who work on safety teams at frontier AI labs: I implore you to make your research publicly available whenever possible, as early as you reasonably can. Suppose that, conditional on a non-doom outcome of AI, there's a 65% chance that the key breakthrough(s) came from within one of the frontier labs. By my estimate, that still means that putting out your work has pretty solid expected value.
I don't care whether you submit it to a conference or format your citations the right way, just get it out there!
Sometimes the preferences people report or even try to demonstrate are better modeled as a political strategy and response to coercion, than as an honest report of intrinsic preferences. Modeling this correctly is important if you want to try to efficiently satisfy others' intrinsic preferences, or even your own. So I'm sharing something I wrote on the topic elsewhere.
You askedwhy people who "believe in" avoiding nonmarital sex so frequently engage in and report badly regretting it. Instead of responding within your frame, I'm going to lay out the interpretive framework that seems most natural to me to use for this problem, and then answer in those terms.

[Summarized Insight] 1. Paul Christiano is working on defining how the question "will AI cause a really bad outcome?" is framed and examining this with probabilities in mind.
2. Eliezer Yudkowsky and Soares have written a book called "If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies," which will be professionally published on September 16th. This book aims to address a broad audience with a message about the potential dangerous trajectories of AI.
3. The book has received positive feedback from early readers who highlight its importance, power of analogy, and call to action regarding AI.
4. Researchers working on safety teams at frontier AI labs are urged to make their research publicly available whenever feasible. Even if the key AI breakthroughs come from these labs, sharing work early and often has significant expected value.
5. A perspective is posited that individuals' reported preferences might be politically strategic responses rather than honest reports of intrinsic preferences, which could impact how efficiently others’ intrinsic preferences are met.
6. A discussion is initiated about why people who claim to believe in avoiding nonmarital sex nonetheless engage in it and often regret it. The author prefers to address this issue using a different interpretive framework.
